skill.name: unity-ecs-patterns
skill.description: Master Unity ECS (Entity Component System) with DOTS, Jobs, and Burst for high-performance game development. Use when building data-oriented games, optimizing performance, or working with large entity counts.
skill.summary: Apply Unity ECS, DOTS, Jobs, and Burst patterns for high-performance gameplay systems.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are markdown and C# Unity ECS examples, not executable skill logic. The only network evidence is three public Unity and GitHub documentation links. No prompt injection, credential access, exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Static command execution alerts are markdown false positives
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to fenced code blocks and inline markdown references, including the resource pointer in SKILL.md and C# snippets in the playbook. I found no shell execution instructions, no generated scripts, and no user-input command interpolation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation or fenced C# examples for Unity ECS. There is no Ruby, shell backtick execution, or executable installer logic in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are Unity ECS terminology false positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts appear to match words such as hash, SpatialHashJob, and OnDestroy in Unity sample code. These examples describe spatial partitioning and ECS lifecycle callbacks, not cryptographic algorithms or security-sensitive hashing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is Unity ECS sample code, including entity destruction and a spatial hash map. No cryptographic API or weak algorithm use is present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are reference documentation links
low_findings.description: The network findings are public links to Unity documentation, Unity sample repositories, and the Burst user guide. They are not active network calls and do not transmit data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plain markdown resource links at the end of the playbook. There is no fetch, curl, telemetry endpoint, or credential-bearing request.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance alerts are normal ECS API examples
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings map to C# Unity API examples for querying entities, counting components, allocating collections, and disposing native containers. I found no host probing, environment inspection, or file system discovery behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The patterns occur in Unity ECS educational examples. They do not inspect the local machine, enumerate files, read environment variables, or perform network reconnaissance.

content.value_statement: Unity DOTS projects can be hard to structure when entity counts and CPU cost grow. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical ECS patterns for components, systems, jobs, Burst, baking, and profiling.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use ECS instead of object-oriented Unity patterns.
Provides component, system, aspect, and singleton examples for Unity DOTS.
Guides Entity Command Buffer usage for deferred structural changes.
Shows Jobs and Burst patterns for parallel entity processing.
Covers baking workflows for converting GameObjects into ECS entities.
Lists performance practices for profiling, chunk usage, and native collection disposal.
limitations: Does not run Unity Editor or verify compiled project output.
Does not replace profiling on target hardware.
Examples may need adjustment for specific Unity Entities package versions.
Focuses on ECS architecture, not rendering, networking, or art pipelines.

use_cases.title: Convert MonoBehaviours to ECS
use_cases.description: Plan component data, systems, and baking steps for moving gameplay logic into DOTS.

use_cases.title: Optimize Large Simulations
use_cases.description: Apply jobs, Burst, entity queries, and native collections to reduce CPU bottlenecks.

use_cases.title: Review ECS Architecture
use_cases.description: Check system boundaries, structural changes, disposal patterns, and query design before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Explain ECS Fit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a new Unity feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain whether this Unity feature should use ECS or MonoBehaviours. Feature: [describe feature]. Include component data, systems, and tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design Components and Systems
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Unity ECS structure for [game mechanic]. List components, systems, queries, command buffers, and data flow.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Bottleneck
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Unity ECS performance problem: [describe bottleneck]. Recommend Jobs, Burst, query, and memory-layout changes.

prompt_templates.title: Audit DOTS Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this Unity DOTS implementation plan for correctness, performance, and maintainability. Focus on structural changes, native collection lifetime, baking, and system ordering.

output_examples.input: I need 5,000 enemies moving toward targets in Unity.
output_examples.output: A concise ECS plan with Speed, Target, Health, and EnemyTag components, a Burst-compatible movement system, and guidance on scheduling parallel work.

output_examples.input: My spawning logic causes sync points and frame spikes.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to batch creation through Entity Command Buffers, separate spawn timing from instantiation, and profile structural changes.

output_examples.input: Convert an EnemyAuthoring MonoBehaviour to DOTS.
output_examples.output: A baking workflow that maps authoring fields to component data and declares prefab dependencies for conversion.

best_practices: Start with plain component data and add aspects only when they simplify repeated access.
Use Entity Command Buffers for structural changes outside hot loops.
Profile on target hardware before adding complex job or memory optimizations.
anti_patterns: Do not use ECS for small features that need object-oriented Unity behavior.
Do not store managed references in Burst-critical component data.
Do not allocate native collections without clear ownership and disposal.

faq.question: What Unity work is this skill best for?
faq.answer: It is best for DOTS architecture, ECS patterns, Jobs, Burst, baking, and high-entity-count gameplay systems.

faq.question: Can it write complete Unity code?
faq.answer: It can draft ECS-oriented code examples and plans, but you must compile and test them in Unity.

faq.question: Does it support MonoBehaviour projects?
faq.answer: It helps compare MonoBehaviour and ECS approaches and can plan migrations where ECS is justified.

faq.question: Does it cover Unity networking?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on local ECS, DOTS, Jobs, Burst, and data-oriented architecture.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it for reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. The prompts support architecture reviews for system ordering, command buffers, and native collection lifetime.

faq.question: Is this safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation examples and public links, with no executable scripts or malicious behavior.
